title: Tools & Repository for the Agentic Organisation
subtitle: From curated document stores to live, machine-readable architecture repositories with automated discovery and AI component registries. How EA content management must evolve.
description: This page covers the Architecture Repository, Enterprise Metamodel, content framework, building blocks, and the new capabilities required for machine-readable content, continuous repository updating, and AI component registries.
sources: TOGAF Architecture Content — FC Part 4, Chapters 1-7
BCG Enterprise as Code — Operating Models as Executable Specifications

sections.label: Content Framework
sections.summary: TOGAF Part 4 provides a comprehensive system for defining, classifying, storing, and governing architecture information. Its layered structure — metamodel, artifacts, deliverables, building blocks, continuum, repository — is one of TOGAF's most extensible contributions.

subsections.title: Framework Structure
subsections.content:
TOGAF's content management operates through a layered architecture with clear separation of concerns:

Metamodel

Defines entities, attributes, and relationships that constitute an enterprise architecture — the vocabulary of architecture.

Artifacts

Tangible outputs of architecture work: Catalogs (lists), Matrices (relationships), Diagrams (visual representations).

Deliverables

Formal work products with defined content and approval processes — contain artifacts, flow through governance.

Building Blocks

ABBs (specifications) and SBBs (implementations) — traceable path from requirements to deployed solutions.

Enterprise Continuum

Classification scheme from generic (foundation) to specific (organisation) — enables systematic reuse.

Repository

Persistent store for all architecture content — seven defined areas, governed access, maintained through ADM cycles.

This layered structure accommodates AI extensions without redesign. It is TOGAF's most extensible contribution and the structural discipline that most EA practices lack.

subsections.title: Architecture Repository — Seven Areas
subsections.content:
Repository AreaPurposeAI-Era Extension
Architecture MetamodelDefines structure and relationships for contentExtend with AI entity types (Agent, Model, Prompt, Interaction)
Architecture CapabilityDocuments the architecture function's own capabilitiesAdd AI-related EA capabilities and tooling
Architecture LandscapeCurrent-state and target-state representationsAdd AI layer showing agents, models, and AI infrastructure
Standards Information BaseApproved standards, references, guidelinesAdd AI governance standards, model evaluation criteria, sovereignty requirements
Reference LibraryTemplates, patterns, reference modelsAdd AI architecture patterns, agent design patterns, reference AI architectures
Governance RepositoryGovernance decisions, compliance assessmentsAdd agent governance decisions, model approvals, sovereignty assessments
Requirements RepositoryRequirements managed throughout ADM cycleAdd AI-specific requirements categories

In addition, two new repository areas are needed: Agent Registry and Model Registry (see AI Component Registries section).

subsections.title: What Changes for Agentic Organisations
subsections.content:
Assumptions That No Longer Hold
  • Content is created by human architects — Significant portions can be auto-discovered from deployment pipelines, cloud APIs, service meshes, and runtime telemetry
  • The metamodel covers what matters — No representation for AI agents, models, prompts, agent interactions, or any AI-specific entity
  • Artifacts are documents for humans — Enterprise as Code requires machine-interpretable content alongside human-readable documentation
  • The repository is curated periodically — AI infrastructure changes weekly, models monthly, agents daily; periodic updates produce perpetually stale content
  • Building blocks are stable and long-lived — AI model SBBs have lifecycles measured in months, not years
What TOGAF Gets Right
  • Content Framework's structural elegance — Separation of metamodel, artifacts, deliverables, building blocks, and repository accommodates AI extensions without redesign
  • Metamodel extensibility — Explicitly designed for extension; AI entities are exactly the kind of extension it was designed to support
  • ABB/SBB distinction — More valuable for AI than traditional tech because AI SBBs change rapidly while ABBs are more stable
  • Enterprise Continuum — Foundation → Common → Industry → Organisation classification applies directly to AI reference architectures
  • Traceability — Business objectives → requirements → ABBs → SBBs → solutions chain extends naturally to AI components

sections.label: Metamodel Extension
sections.summary: The Enterprise Metamodel needs five core AI entities added as first-class citizens. TOGAF's metamodel was designed for extension — the mechanism is exactly right, only the AI-specific content is missing.

subsections.title: Five Core AI Entities
subsections.content:
EntityKey AttributesRelationships
AI AgentIdentity, role, capabilities, access permissions, decision authority, accountable human owner, deployed models, communication protocols, lifecycle statusUses → AI Model, Implements → Business Capability, Governed by → Architecture Principle, Owned by → Actor
AI ModelIdentity, provider, version, task type, cost per inference, accuracy, latency, deployment location, sovereignty constraints, deprecation dateUsed by → AI Agent, Deployed on → Technology Component, Constrained by → Sovereignty Policy
Prompt TemplateIdentity, purpose, associated model(s), version, owner, effectiveness metricsUsed by → AI Agent, Optimised for → AI Model, Produces → Business Output
Agent InteractionSource agent, target agent, protocol (MCP/A2A/ACP), data exchanged, frequency, purposeConnects → AI Agent to AI Agent, Uses → Communication Protocol
Model DeploymentModel identity, infrastructure, location, scaling config, cost, performance SLADeploys → AI Model, Runs on → Technology Component, Governed by → SLA

These entities create the vocabulary for discussing AI architecture consistently across the enterprise. Do not wait for tool support — a spreadsheet or simple database is sufficient to start.

subsections.title: New Artifact Types
subsections.content:
The catalog/matrix/diagram taxonomy is durable. AI architecture needs new instances within these categories:

CategoryNew ArtifactPurpose
CatalogAgent CatalogueAll agents with role, capabilities, access, owner, status, performance
CatalogModel Portfolio CatalogAll models with provider, version, task fit, cost, accuracy, lifecycle
MatrixAgent/Capability MatrixMapping agents to business capabilities they support
MatrixModel/Infrastructure MatrixMapping models to deployment infrastructure and locations
MatrixSovereignty Compliance MatrixAI workloads mapped to sovereignty constraints and deployment rules
DiagramAgent Interaction DiagramAgent-to-agent communication, delegation patterns, data flows
DiagramAI Infrastructure DiagramAI compute, storage, model serving, agent runtime architecture
GovernanceModel Decision LogModel selection decisions with rationale, alternatives, review schedule
GovernanceAI Cost Allocation MatrixInference, training, infrastructure costs mapped to business capabilities

subsections.title: AI Building Blocks
subsections.content:
The ABB/SBB distinction maps directly to AI architecture — and becomes more important because AI SBBs are expected to be interchanged frequently as the model market evolves:

TypeABB (Specification)SBB (Implementation)
Agent"Customer Query Classifier: categorise queries by intent with >90% accuracy, <200ms latency, 1000 req/min""GPT-4o-mini with custom prompt, Azure OpenAI eu-west-1, RAG over knowledge base X"
Model"Summarisation model: documents up to 50K tokens, extractive + abstractive, <$0.01 per summary""Claude 3.5 Haiku via API, $0.003 per 1K output tokens"
Infrastructure"Model serving platform: auto-scaling, <100ms p99 latency, multi-model routing""AWS SageMaker with Inferentia2, deployed in eu-west-1"

The ABB-to-SBB mapping is the architectural mechanism for model portability. When a better or cheaper model appears, you change the SBB while the ABB remains stable. This is the foundation for avoiding vendor lock-in.

sections.label: Machine-Readable Content
sections.summary: Enterprise as Code requires architecture content that serves two audiences: human architects who need readable documentation, and automated systems that need structured data. Architecture standards must become code, not documents.

subsections.title: Enterprise as Code Vision
subsections.content:
BCG's Enterprise as Code vision proposes that implicit operating models — the unwritten rules, workflows, decision logic, and governance processes — must be codified as executable specifications:

  • Architecture policies as code — deployment standards, technology constraints, and security requirements in policy-as-code languages (Open Policy Agent/Rego, AWS Config Rules, Azure Policy)
  • Architecture metamodel as data — metamodel content in structured formats (YAML, JSON, RDF) that automated tools can query and validate
  • Architecture standards as API — programmatic access to the Standards Information Base so deployment pipelines check compliance automatically
  • Dual-format content — single source of truth generates both human-readable documentation and machine-readable specifications

"Freedom within a Frame" — BCG's model proposes centralised architectural guardrails expressed as code, with decentralised innovation operating freely within those guardrails. This reportedly cuts costs by up to 30% and improves time-to-market by 50%.

subsections.title: Dual-Format Content Production
subsections.content:
The gap between architecture intent (human-readable) and architecture enforcement (machine-readable) is the primary reason standards are routinely violated — enforcement depends on human reviewers who cannot check every deployment.

Content TypeHuman-Readable FormatMachine-Readable Format
Architecture principlesDocument with rationale and implicationsStructured YAML with testable assertions
Technology standardsStandards catalogue with guidancePolicy-as-code rules enforceable in CI/CD
Compliance rulesChecklist with pass/fail criteriaAutomated compliance checks (OPA, Config Rules)
Architecture patternsPattern documentation with diagramsInfrastructure-as-code templates, reference architectures
Deployment policiesGovernance handbook sectionAdmission controllers, pipeline gates
Architecture landscapeDiagrams and narrative descriptionsAuto-discovered data in structured APIs

subsections.title: Maturity Model
subsections.content:
LevelDescription
Level 1All architecture content is human-readable only (documents, wikis, diagrams). No machine-readable content exists.
Level 2Some standards expressed as code (technology standards, deployment rules). Repository accessible via basic API. Mix of manual and automated compliance.
Level 3Architecture policies systematically expressed as code. Metamodel content available as structured data. Key artifacts published in dual format. Technology compliance primarily automated.
Level 4Dual-format content production from single source of truth. Full repository API access. AI agents consume content for analysis, compliance, and decision support.
Level 5Content is natively machine-readable with human views generated on demand. AI agents participate in architecture development. Content is an executable specification — Enterprise as Code realised.

sections.label: AI Component Registries
sections.summary: Organisations deploying AI at scale cannot govern what they cannot see. The Agent Registry and Model Registry are new first-class areas of the Architecture Repository — foundational infrastructure for AI-era EA.

subsections.title: Agent Registry
subsections.content:
A dedicated registry of all AI agents deployed in the enterprise. This is the prerequisite for every other agent governance capability — you cannot govern, optimise, or secure what you cannot see.

Attribute CategoryFields
IdentityAgent ID, name, version, deployment date, lifecycle status (testing/staging/production/deprecated/retired)
CapabilityRole description, task type, capabilities, decision authority level, escalation rules
AccountabilityAccountable human owner, responsible team, governance tier, last review date
TechnicalDeployed models, communication protocols (MCP/A2A/ACP), infrastructure, deployment location
Access & SecurityData inputs/outputs, access permissions, sovereignty constraints, security classification
PerformanceAccuracy metrics, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rates, cost per interaction, throughput

Many organisations cannot answer "How many AI agents do we have?" — the Agent Registry answers this question and enables all subsequent governance, optimisation, and security capabilities.

subsections.title: Model Registry
subsections.content:
A dedicated registry of all AI models used across the enterprise. Enables model portfolio management, cost optimisation, and vendor risk management.

Attribute CategoryFields
IdentityModel ID, provider, version, task type, capability description
PerformanceAccuracy metrics by task, latency characteristics, throughput capacity
CostCost per inference, monthly spend, cost trend, budget allocation
DeploymentDeployment locations, scaling configuration, infrastructure requirements
ComplianceSovereignty constraints, data residency, regulatory classification (EU AI Act risk tier)
LifecycleContract terms, SLA commitments, planned deprecation date, alternative models evaluated

subsections.title: Registry Maturity Model
subsections.content:
LevelDescription
Level 1No centralised registry. Agents and models deployed ad hoc with no enterprise visibility. Individual teams may track their own deployments.
Level 2Basic registry established. All agents and models documented with core attributes. Registry manually maintained with quarterly updates.
Level 3Comprehensive registry with full attributes. Automated data collection for some attributes (cost, performance from provider APIs). Registration required before production deployment.
Level 4Registry continuously updated from runtime data. Automated governance: policy checks, cost alerts, performance degradation detection. Model portfolio optimisation informed by registry data.
Level 5Registry is the authoritative control plane for AI component lifecycle. Onboarding, selection, performance management, and retirement all flow through the registry. Automated lifecycle management based on registry policies.

sections.label: Continuous Repository
sections.summary: The shift from 'architecture as documentation' to 'architecture as continuous discovery'. When deployment pipelines report what was deployed and cloud APIs report what exists, the as-is architecture maintains itself automatically.

subsections.title: Automated Data Sources
subsections.content:
Data SourceContent UpdatedUpdate Frequency
Cloud inventory APIsInfrastructure landscape — compute, storage, network, managed servicesNear-real-time
CI/CD pipeline metadataApplication deployment state, version history, deployment topologyOn each deployment
Service mesh telemetryIntegration topology — which services communicate, data flows, protocolsContinuous
API gateway configurationService endpoints, API contracts, traffic routingOn configuration change
Kubernetes/container orchestrationApplication deployment, scaling, resource allocationContinuous
IaC state filesInfrastructure definitions, drift from declared stateOn apply
CMDB integrationAsset inventory, ownership, support informationPeriodic sync
Cost management APIsFinOps data — spend by service, trend analysis, budget trackingDaily/hourly

subsections.title: Drift Detection & Compliance
subsections.content:
Continuous monitoring that alerts when deployed state diverges from target architecture:

  • Configuration drift — infrastructure deviates from IaC definitions or approved patterns
  • Standards drift — deployed technologies or versions fall outside approved standards
  • Architecture drift — system communication patterns deviate from designed integration topology
  • Compliance drift — deployments violate sovereignty, security, or regulatory constraints
  • Cost drift — AI inference or infrastructure costs exceed defined thresholds

Drift detection transforms Phase G (Implementation Governance) from periodic compliance reviews to continuous automated monitoring with exception-based human intervention.

subsections.title: Repository Maturity Model
subsections.content:
LevelDescription
Level 1All content manually curated. Repository updated during architecture reviews. Significant portions stale or incomplete.
Level 2Some automated discovery for cloud infrastructure. IaC state files provide partial infrastructure view. Other domains manual.
Level 3Automated discovery for infrastructure, deployment state, and integration topology. Manual effort focused on business architecture and application logic. Basic drift detection.
Level 4Comprehensive automated discovery across all technology domains. Near-real-time updates. Full drift detection with automated alerting. Repository accessible via APIs.
Level 5Full digital twin of the enterprise technology landscape maintained automatically. Discovery feeds drive automated compliance, gap analysis, and change impact. Human architects work exclusively at design and strategy level.

sections.label: Enterprise Continuum for AI
sections.summary: The Enterprise Continuum's classification scheme — from generic to organisation-specific — applies directly to AI architecture assets. Populate each level with AI reference architectures, agent patterns, and model selection criteria for systematic reuse.

subsections.title: AI Asset Classification
subsections.content:
LevelTraditional ContentAI Architecture Content
FoundationGeneric architectures (TOGAF TRM)Multi-agent orchestration patterns, standard RAG architecture, model abstraction layer, agent communication patterns
Common SystemsShared across industriesAI platform architectures, model serving patterns, agent lifecycle management approaches
Industry-SpecificVertical-specific architecturesIndustry-specific AI use cases, domain-specific agent patterns, sector sovereignty requirements
Organisation-SpecificEnterprise-tailoredCustom agent implementations, fine-tuned models, organisation-specific AI governance rules

subsections.title: AI Architecture Section in Deliverables
subsections.content:
Architecture Definition Documents for AI-related initiatives should include a dedicated AI Architecture section:

Agent Architecture

Which agents, what roles, how they interact, delegation patterns, escalation rules

Model Architecture

Which models, what tasks, how they are managed, portability requirements, cost optimisation

AI Infrastructure

Compute, storage, platform services, serving infrastructure, scaling approach

AI Governance

Policies, compliance mechanisms, accountability structures, monitoring, audit trails

sections.label: Practitioner Guidance
sections.summary: Concrete actions for EA practitioners to evolve their architecture content management from periodic human-curated documentation to continuous, machine-readable, AI-aware repositories.

phases.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Extend your metamodel with five core AI entities (Agent, Model, Prompt Template, Agent Interaction, Model Deployment) — a spreadsheet is sufficient to start.
Create an Agent Registry — document every deployed AI agent with role, data access, decision authority, owner, location, and performance metrics.
Create a Model Registry — document every AI model in use with provider, version, cost, accuracy, deployment location, and sovereignty status.
Audit your repository for staleness — assess each of the seven areas for currency, identify which areas are so stale they are actively misleading.

phases.title: Near-Term Actions
actions: Develop new artifact templates: Agent Catalogue, Model Portfolio Matrix, Agent Interaction Diagram, AI Infrastructure Diagram, Sovereignty Compliance Matrix.
Define AI Building Blocks — establish ABB/SBB patterns for agents and models, creating the architectural mechanism for model portability.
Connect one automated data source to your repository — cloud inventory API, IaC state files, or CI/CD pipeline metadata for the most dynamic content area.
Add AI governance content to the Standards Information Base — model evaluation criteria, agent communication standards, sovereignty requirements, AI ethics principles.
Populate the Enterprise Continuum with AI assets — foundation-level patterns, industry-specific references, and organisation-specific implementations.

phases.title: Medium-Term Actions
actions: Implement machine-readable architecture content — express standards and compliance rules in policy-as-code, expose the metamodel as structured data APIs.
Implement continuous repository updating for technology domains — automated feeds from cloud APIs, service meshes, CI/CD pipelines, with drift detection.
Extend the Enterprise Continuum for AI assets — populate each level (Foundation, Common, Industry, Organisation) with AI architecture patterns and reference implementations.
Create an AI Architecture section in Architecture Definition Documents — covering agent, model, infrastructure, and governance architecture.
Pilot a digital architecture twin for one domain — comprehensive automated repository maintaining near-real-time representation of deployed state.
